coredata.dc:title: Algae biomass as a precursor for synthesis of nitrogen-and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots: A better probe in Arabidopsis guard cells and root tissues
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Che
Xiao, Yumei
Ma, Yongqiang
Li, Baoming
Liu, Zhidan
Lu, Cheng
Liu, Xue
Wei, Yao
Zhu, Zhangbing
Zhang, Yuanhui
coredata.dc:description: The challenge of controlling algal blooms and reusing algal biomass remain unsolved worldwide. We introduce a facile method to reuse Nannochloropsis biocrude oil (NBO) for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N-S-C-dots). N-S-C-dots can pass through the heavily thickened wall of mature Arabidopsis thaliana (A. thaliana) guard cells because of high solubility and excellent biocompatibility. N-S-C-dots exhibit multicolor luminescence and could effectively reduce the interference of autofluorescence in plant cells by changing filters. Bioimaging of root tissues reveals that 2 major factors affect the transmission of N-S-C-dots: high osmotic pressure and intensity of cellular metabolism. This study highlights the potential application of CDs for bioimaging in plant cells and demonstrates the significance of investigating the reuse of algal biomass.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Algae biomass
Carbon dots
Bioimaging
Guard cells
Multicolor luminescence

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) C1s spectrum, (c) N1s spectrum (d) S2p spectrum of the N-S-C-dots.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) X-ray diffraction patterns of the N-S-C-dots.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the N-S-C-dots. The inset in (a) demonstrates a particle size histogram.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (b) PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths, increasing from 280nm to 560nm in 20nm increments. Insets in (a): photos of the aqueous solution of the N-S-C-dots under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Representative laser scanning confocal microscopy images in guard cells: (a) four crescent guard cells, (b) DAPI (blue), (c) FITC (green), (d) R-DIL (red) and (e) bright-field. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Representative fluorescence images in root tissues under an excitation wavelength of 405nm: (a) root hairs, (b) lateral roots, (c) cells of elongation zone, (d) vascular bundles, (e) vacuoles (dark areas). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Representative fluorescence images in lateral roots incubated with the N-S-C-dots at the different temperatures under an excitation wavelength of 405nm: 4°C for (a) and 20°C for (b).

Comparison of FM4-64 and N-S-C-dots: (a) red luminescence of FM4-64 under excitation of 514nm, (b) blue fluorescence image of N-S-C-dots under excitation of 405nm, (c) bright-field. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Quantum yields and lifetimes of carbon dots prepared from NBO by different routes.

row.entry: Heating under vigorous stir in concentrated sulfuric acid
The Maillard reaction and further carbonization in concentrated sulfuric acid

head.ce:title: Algae biomass as a precursor for synthesis of nitrogen-and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots: A better probe in Arabidopsis guard cells and root tissues

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, College of Science, China Agricultural University, No.2 West Yuanmingyuan Road, Beijing 100193, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical, Materials and Mechatronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Agricultural and Biological Engineering, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, Urbana, IL 61801, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Agricultural and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The challenge of controlling algal blooms and reusing algal biomass remain unsolved worldwide. We introduce a facile method to reuse Nannochloropsis biocrude oil (NBO) for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N-S-C-dots). N-S-C-dots can pass through the heavily thickened wall of mature Arabidopsis thaliana (A. thaliana) guard cells because of high solubility and excellent biocompatibility. N-S-C-dots exhibit multicolor luminescence and could effectively reduce the interference of autofluorescence in plant cells by changing filters. Bioimaging of root tissues reveals that 2 major factors affect the transmission of N-S-C-dots: high osmotic pressure and intensity of cellular metabolism. This study highlights the potential application of CDs for bioimaging in plant cells and demonstrates the significance of investigating the reuse of algal biomass.

ce:section.ce:para: Eutrophication directly results in algal blooms and has drawn global interest [1]. Algal blooms lead to anoxia and exert harmful effects on fishery resources, ecosystems, and human health [2]. Algal biomass reserves massive energy through photosynthesis [3]. Combustion of fossil fuels and environmental issues have directed the attention to conversion of algal biomass into sustainable renewable biofuels [4,5]. Hydrothermal liquefaction (HTL) can be effectively used to convert algal biomass into biocrude oil [6–8]. Nannochloropsis biocrude oil (NBO) contains 7.5% nitrogen after HTL [8]. Given that nitrogen removal is time-consuming and labor-intensive, biocrude with such high nitrogen content could limit its wider applicability as energy [6]. However, it provides an excellent environment to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon dots, as determined after several trials. This advantage may encourage recycling high-nitrogen algal biomass to reduce the pollution of algal blooms and protect the environment.

Carbon dots can be applied as fluorescent probes for bioimaging because of stable photoluminescence, high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [9–11]. Unfortunately, most target cells, including HeLa cells, are animal cells [12,13]. Imaging of mice in vivo was conducted after subcutaneous injection [14]. Imaging experiments on plant cells are rarely conducted. Compared with animal cells, plant cells exhibit more complex cell structures, including cell walls, vacuoles, chloroplasts, and fibrous tissues. Guard cells have been used as one of the most frequently used model systems to study signal transduction and cell shape determination in plants. However, the difficulty in introducing the appropriate probes into guard cells is that some conventional probes such as antibodies or peptide stains fail to easily pass through the heavily thickened wall of the mature guard cell. Researchers have attempted to overcome this problem via 2 methods: microinjection [15] and frozen immunoprocedures [16]. However, the procedure entails challenge as the central region of the guard cells is occupied by a large vacuole and a nucleus, leaving the cytoplasm with a considerably small room for microinjection. In addition, freezing conditions and mechanical forces are likely to damage the cell structure [17]. Another obstacle to bioimaging is autofluorescence, which is widely exhibited by animal and plant cells and may directly affect the observation [18]. An appropriate probe with multicolor fluorescence at different excitation wavelengths may help eliminate the interference of other compounds in plant cells [19].

The current study demonstrates that NBO can be used as a superior precursor for the preparation of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (N-S-C-dots) via the Maillard reaction [20,21] facilitated by concentrated sulfuric acid. The prepared N-S-C-dots exhibit luminescence stability, high solubility, and good biocompatibility. As a probe for guard cells, N-S-C-dots can pass through the heavily thickened wall easily and quickly. The fluorescence images in guard cells associated with the N-S-C-dots exhibit strong blue, green, and red fluorescence, which could effectively avoid autofluorescence in other cell structures in guard cells. In addition, imaging experiments on roots reveal the distribution of N-S-C-dots in root tissues and may help detect the metabolism of the root tissue. All results confirm their potential for application in plant labeling and imaging.

Nannochloropsis was provided by the Laboratory of Environment-Enhancing Energy, China Agricultural University (China), concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) was purchased from MREDA Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, U.S.A.). All other reagents employed were analytically pure as purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing CO., Ltd. A. thaliana plants were grown for 3–4weeks under a cycle of 12h light (170μmolphotonm−2 s−1) and 12h dark. The air temperature was kept at 25°C during the day and 22°C at night. Leaf discs (6mmdiameter) were cut from fully expanded leaves and guard cells were taken from the lower leaf side. Root tissues were obtained from the mature A. thaliana.

NBO was prepared by the HTL procedures, the specific method and the organic composition of the biocrude oil refer to our previous literature [8]. In a typical procedure, 1.0g of NBO was added into a 25mL one-neck flask. The vessel was placed in the microwave (750W) for 30min for a quick Maillard reaction as an activation process. After that, brown nitrogenous polymers were obtained. Then 0.5mL of concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) was dropped into the NBO under vigorous stir for further carbonization. The mixture was stirred for 30min at 80°C then diluted with 20mL of deionized water, followed by adjusting the pH to 7 with NaOH. The suspension was filtered through a 0.22-lm membrane to remove large carbon residues, a dark brown solution of N-S-C-dots was obtained. Further purification was conducted through a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut off: 1000Da) for 5h (changed deionized water every 1h). 0.25g of purified N-S-C-dots was finally obtained.

Guard cells and root tissues were incubated with the N-S-C-dots at a concentration of 300μgml−1 for 3h respectively. After that, both materials were washed thoroughly three times with deionized water to remove the excess N-S-C-dots. Cellular internalization of N-S-C-dots and expression were detected using a confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica TCS SP5) through three different filters: DAPI, FITC and R-DIL.

Several characterization methods were used to confirm the morphology and structure of the N-S-C-dots. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was taken on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was recorded on a PerkinElmer 2000 in the range of 400–4000cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) and a BrukerD/MAX 2400 X-ray diffractometer (XRD) were both used for structure analysis. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were carried out on a JEOL 2100. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrophotometer. Cell imaging was done under Leica TCS SP5 confocal microscope. Thermogravimetric analysis was carried out on a thermal analyzer (STA 449, F3 Jupiter) by heating the sample (5mg) from room temperature to 800°C (15°Cmin−1) under a N2 atmosphere.

The quantum yield was calculated according to the method using quinine sulfate as the standard and calculated with following equation [22]:
QY X
=
QY std
I x
/
I std
A std
/
A x
η x 2
/
η std 2
where the subscript “x” indicates the unknown sample, the subscript “std” is quinine sulfate, “QY” stands for the quantum yield, “I” is the integrated PL intensity, “A” is the absorbance, and “η” stands for the refractive index of the solvent. Quinine sulfate (quantum yield: 54%) was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (refractive index: 1.33) and the N-S-C-dots were dissolved in water (refractive index: 1.33). In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance value of the solution was kept below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength (360nm).

XPS was used to determine the atomic composition. Fig. 1a shows the XPS spectra of N-S-C-dots, which shows sulfur (around 164eV), carbon (around 285eV), nitrogen (around 398.5eV), and oxygen (around 531eV) atoms, as well as some metal atoms: Ca (around 352eV) and Mg (around 1305eV). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1b) exhibits five distinct carbon states: 284.5, 285.3, 286 and 286.5eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, CS, CN, CO species, respectively [23]. The N1s spectrum has three peaks at 398.9, 399.7 and 401.1eV, corresponding to the pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphite N, respectively (Fig. 1c) [24]. The peak located at 167.9eV suggest only one form of sulfur, which deconvoluted into three components, arising from a CSOx (x=2, 3, 4) species [25]. These results reveal that the most sulfur atoms are functionalized the surface of the carbon dots in the state of sulfonate. The N, Ma and Ca contained in the N-S-C-dots come from the precursor [8], while the S comes from the sulfuric acid. The elemental analysis demonstrates the composition of the N-S-C-dots is C 53.25wt%, S 3.87wt%, N 4.41wt%, O 30.72wt%, Mg 3.31wt%, Ca 1.92wt%, agreeing well with the XPS test. The FTIR spectra were acquired to identify the functional groups in the N-S-C-dots (Fig. 2a). The broad band at 3400cm−1 corresponds to the OH and NH stretching vibration [26]. The peak at 1643cm−1 is the CO stretching vibration. The peak around 1384cm−1 can be identified as CN, NH groups [27]. The peak at 1123cm−1 is assigned to the CO, CN, and CS bonds [28]. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2b) displays a broad, low-intensity diffraction peak centered at 23.7°, revealing an amorphous carbon phase. Fig. 3a shows N-S-C-dots are spherical and monodispersed. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 3a, inset) shows they have a narrow size distribution between 2.5 and 6.0nm with an average diameter of 4.0nm. Fig. 3b shows an HRTEM image of a typical N-S-C-dot and it exhibits a lattice spacing of approximately 0.30nm, which corresponds to the [002] facet of graphite carbon [29].

In order to explore the optical properties of N-S-C-dots, PL and UV–Vis absorption spectra were shown in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a insets, the aqueous solution of N-S-C-dots (left) is transparent and yellowish in daylight and under excitation of 365nm UV light (right) it shows bright blue luminescence. N-S-C-dots dispersed in water have a featureless UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4a). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements confirmed that the bright fluorescence originated from N-S-C-dots. With an increase in the excitation wavelength from 280nm to 560nm, the emission from N-S-C-dots shifted to higher wavelengths gradually by a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4b). The emission spectra of N-S-C-dots was broad with a dependence on the excitation wavelengths, which could explain these colors were observed through three different filters: DAPI (blue), FITC (green) and R-DIL (red).

Four crescent guard cells treated with N-S-C-dots show intense blue luminescence whether the stomata is open or closed (Fig. 5a), indicating N-S-C-dots successfully entered into the guard cells. Guard cells are responsible for controlling the penetration of gas and water. When guard cells absorb water, the stomata is open. When guard cells lose water, the stomata is closed. The constant transfer of water can cause the change of internal pressure, which can lead to the movement of stomatal. The excellent water solubility ensures that N-S-C-dots can penetrate the cell walls of guard cells smoothly with the water diffusion. On the other hand, N-S-C-dots are derived from biomass algae with good biocompatibility, which are less likely to be rejected by the cell membrane. Compared to the bright-field image (Fig. 5e), the fluorescence images exhibit strongly blue (Fig. 5b), green (Fig. 5c), and red (Fig. 5d) fluorescence through three different filters: DAPI (excitation wavelength 405nm), FITC (excitation wavelength 488nm) and R-DIL (excitation wavelength 560nm). There is an obvious target with similar size to normal guard cells on the top left of Fig. 5b. However, this blue object under DAPI is not a guard cell according to observation in bright-field (Fig. 5e). We can also determine that this is a disruptor with autofluorescence by changing filters, which cannot be observed at FITC (Fig. 5c) or R-DIL (Fig. 5d). Because the observation of a wide range of distribution and statistics need a smaller magnification microscope, in this case, observers are not able to see the cell shape and structure clearly. It may be unreliable to eliminate interference by simple comparison between fluorescent images and bright field. N-S-C-dots with multicolor fluorescence contributes to eliminate the interference of other compounds by changing different filters since the autofluorescence is generally observed under single excitation in plant cells [19].

FM4-64 is one the most commonly used membrane-selective dyes to visualize membrane internalization and transport [31]. FM4-64 and N-S-C-dots are simultaneously used as probes in the root tissue of A. thaliana. Simultaneous confocal fluorescence images and corresponding brightfield images are collected (Fig. 8 ). FM4-64 shows intense red luminescence under excitation of 514nm (Fig. 8a), but irregular dispersion and partial aggregation are observed, which may be caused by excessive cellular structure and cell membrane folds. The complexity of the root tissue suggests that choosing FM4-64 as a probe is not a wise choice. By contrast, there is a better dispersion in fluorescence image of N-S-C-dots under excitation of 405nm (Fig. 8b). The shape of rectangular cells are clearly visible and aggregation is rarely observed. Comparison results indicate N-S-C-dots can be used as a better probe for bioimaging in the complicated root tissue of A. thaliana.

The stability of the N-S-C-dots was investigated by the effects of UV exposure, ionic strength, pH from 1 to 14 and thermogravemetric analysis (TGA). There is no obvious decline of PL intensity under continuous UV excitation for 4hours (Fig. S1a). The fluorescent property of the N-S-C-dots at different ionic strengths was quite stable while increasing the concentration of NaCl solution from 0 to 1.0M (Fig. S1b). The stability of the N-S-C-dots in high-salt conditions ensures their applications in root tissue of plants as plant root cells usually maintain a high osmotic pressure to absorb water from the soil [32]. PL intensities decrease in solutions with higher or lower pH and the highest PL intensity is recorded in neutral aqueous solution (Fig. S1c). The presence of NH and COO– groups in the N-S-C-dots may cause this trend [13]. N-S-C-dots are easily dissolved in water, methanol, isopropanol, ethylene glycol and acetone, while hardly dissolved in ethyl acetate, toluene and cyclohexane (Fig. S1d), which indicates a high solubility in polar organic solvents. TGA results were shown in Fig. S2. Two stepwise weight-loss cycles at 152 and 510°C were observed. The initial weight loss of 6.5% at 152°C is mainly attributed to evaporation of water molecules attached via weak hydrogen bonding within the N-S-C-dots [33]. The second weight loss of 29.1% until 510°C may be caused by the decomposition of organic functional groups in N-S-C-dots [34].The temperatures of 8% weight loss are more than 200°C, which prove good thermal stability.

Various precursors have been employed for the preparation of C-dots, including graphene [35], saccharides [36], amino acid [37], citric acid [38], candle soot [39], natural gas soot [40], and waste frying oil [41]. Compared with other precursors, NBO has unique advantages: being feedstocks from nature with high photosynthetic efficiency, non-arable land use, and numerous environmental benefits [8]. NBO also contains a high level of nitrogen, and nitrogen-doped C-dots are widely reported because the relatively strong electron affinity of N atoms may restrict electron delocalization and contribute to the PL shift [26,42,43]. When the excitation wavelength changes from 280nm to 560nm, the corresponding PL emission peak for the N-S-C-dots (Fig. 1b) shifts from 400nm to 550nm. This occurrence allows for the emission of multiple colors by changing 3 filters [13]. This ability is important for bioimaging applications, which can avoid harmful UV and blue excitations caused by autofluorescence [19].

Although biomass is a highly important source of precursors, most researchers have to find appropriate biomass materials instead of creating them. By HTL and the Maillard reaction, a suitable intermediate may be obtained for conversion from various biomass materials into carbon-based materials. First, HTL allows direct conversion from various biomass materials into biocrude oil [44]. These biomass materials include hardwoods such as white poplar [45], softwoods such as sugarcane bagasse, agricultural residues such as sugarcane bagasse, and other wastes such as newspaper [46]. The Maillard reaction requires several conditions, including temperature, pH, and oxygen [47]. It continues to occur in both HTL and the preparation of N-S-C-dots. The former requires high temperature and high pressure for algae conversion, whereas the latter is used for further carbonization at microwave and a relatively lower temperature. The reaction is mainly composed of carbohydrate compounds, amino acids, and phenolic compounds. The Maillard reaction starts with condensation between the aldehydes of reducing saccharides and the amino-containing proteins or amino acids, resulting in brown nitrogenous polymers [48,49]. These nitrogenous polymers with diverse chemical features as well as surface defects and cores with an electronic structure can be used effectively for further preparation [50].

The Maillard reaction provides valuable support; however, optimization of N-S-C-dot preparation indicates that concentrated sulfuric acid is also indispensable. The experimental results are listed in Table 1 . The fluorescence lifetimes of the carbon dots prepared using 3 different methods were measured by time-correlated single photon counting, with excitation and emission wavelengths of 340 and 430nm. The PL quantum yield of 3 different carbon dots was measured using quinine sulfate as a reference. Those prepared using the combined method exhibited the longest lifetime (4.37ns). The quantum yield of carbon dots prepared by the Maillard reaction alone was 6.92%, which almost doubled to 13.71% with concentrated sulfuric acid. The yield was higher than those of other precursors, including polyethylene glycol (3.5%) [51], waste frying oil (3.7%) [12], and hair [13] (11.1%). Concentrated sulfuric acid was added to the preparation process for its strong dehydrating and oxidizing properties [9]. However, the carbon dots heated under vigorous stirring in concentrated sulfuric acid exhibited the shortest fluorescence lifetime and lowest quantum yield. Concentrated sulfuric acid is inefficient for carbonization from complex precursors such as saccharides and the amino-containing proteins in NBO. Fortunately, the Maillard reaction facilitated the conversion of carbohydrates into nitrogen-containing polymers [50], which can be used effectively by concentrated sulfuric acid for further carbonization.

In conclusion, NBO was used as a green precursor converted by HTL to prepare sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots via the Maillard reaction with concentrated sulfuric acid. The N-S-C-dots exhibit excellent multicolor luminescence via 3 different filters, which possess good luminescence stability and high solubility. Bioimaging of guard cells and root tissues confirm that as-synthesized N-S-C-dots can be potentially used as bio-labeling agents for plant cells. Success in converting algal biomass into carbon dots suggests that carbon sources can meet our needs from diverse biomass.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was supported by a grant (2015BAK45B01) from Ministry of Science and Technology. We appreciated the support from Dabeinong Educational Fund. Dr. Zhidan Liu was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (U1562107). The authors also thank the coordination from Collaborative Innovation Center for Energy Economics and Energy Policy, Xiamen University.
